title: Check Point Threat Prevention Extraction Blocks
spl:
index=security sourcetype="cp:threatprevention" action=prevent
| stats count by protection_type, severity
| sort -count
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks by IDS_Attacks.severity | sort - count
description: Threat prevention blocks show inline protection value across blades.
value: Threat prevention blocks show inline protection value across blades.
implementation: Aggregate IPS, AV, and AB combined preventions. Prioritize by severity and asset.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_checkpoint`.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=cp:threatprevention`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Aggregate IPS, AV, and AB combined preventions. Prioritize by severity and asset.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=security sourcetype="cp:threatprevention" action=prevent
| stats count by protection_type, severity
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Check Point Threat Prevention Extraction Blocks** ‚Äî Threat prevention blocks show inline protection value across blades.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cp:threatprevention`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_checkpoint`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: security; **sourcetype**: cp:threatprevention. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP ‚Äî interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=security, sourcetype="cp:threatprevention". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by protection_type, severity** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks by IDS_Attacks.severity | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Check Point Threat Prevention Extraction Blocks** ‚Äî Threat prevention blocks show inline protection value across blades.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cp:threatprevention`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_checkpoint`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Detection type** for this use case: TTP ‚Äî interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Intrusion_Detection.IDS_Attacks` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked bar (types), Table.
knownFalsePositives: Legitimate use of dual-purpose administrative tools (e.g. PowerShell, SSH, cloud CLIs) during maintenance or deployment can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change tickets, asset criticality and user role before escalating.
